skill.name: NIH Biosketch Builder
skill.description: Generate NIH-compliant biosketch documents in DOCX format using the 2022 OMB-approved template and PubMed publication data import.
skill.summary: Automatically generate formatted NIH biosketch documents for grant applications from structured JSON data with PubMed integration.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 77 potential issues with a computed risk score of 100/100, but evaluation confirmed the vast majority are false positives: 32 markdown backtick code-formatting instances misclassified as shell execution, 19 occurrences of the word 'description' misclassified as weak cryptography, and 7 text matches in a JSON audit report misclassified as system reconnaissance. After evaluation, only legitimate capabilities remain: network access to the NIH PubMed API for publication data import, filesystem writes for DOCX/JSON document generation, and an executable Python script that is transparent and well-structured. No credential theft, data exfiltration, command injection, prompt injection, or malicious intent detected. The skill serves its documented academic writing purpose.

low_findings.title: Network Access to External API
low_findings.description: Skill makes HTTP requests to eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov (NCBI PubMed API) to import publication data. The endpoint is a legitimate US government research service. Network access is documented in the skill's README and is essential to the publication import feature.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Write Operations for Document Generation
low_findings.description: Skill writes JSON and DOCX files to user-specified output paths provided via command-line arguments. These writes serve the core document-generation purpose. No path traversal sanitization is implemented, but the output path is explicitly provided by the user as a CLI argument.

low_findings.title: Executable Python Script with User-Controlled Input
low_findings.description: Skill contains a 669-line Python script that parses CLI arguments, reads JSON input files, makes network requests, and writes output files. The script uses only standard library modules and well-known packages (python-docx, requests). No obfuscation, encoded payloads, or suspicious patterns detected.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: 32 instances of markdown code formatting using backticks in SKILL.md and references/audit-reference.md were incorrectly flagged as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are standard markdown syntax for inline code and fenced code blocks, not actual command execution. The skill uses Python (not Ruby) and does not invoke subprocesses or shell commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: 'description' Keyword Flagged as Weak Cryptography
dangerous_patterns.description: 19 instances of the word 'description' in scripts/main.py and SKILL.md were flagged as weak cryptographic algorithm by the static analyzer. The word appears as a Python dictionary key for data model fields, JSON property names, and argparse help text. None of these occurrences relate to cryptography, hashing, or encryption in any way.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Path Traversal Mention in Security Checklist
dangerous_patterns.description: Line 175 of SKILL.md contains '../' as part of a security checklist item reading 'No unauthorized file system access (../)'. This is documentation describing what the skill should NOT do, not an actual path traversal attempt.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Audit Report Text Flagged as System Reconnaissance
dangerous_patterns.description: 7 lines in nih-biosketch-builder_audit_result_v2.json were flagged as system reconnaissance. This file is a pre-existing JSON audit report containing evaluation results and commentary text. No system commands, reconnaissance patterns, or executable code exist in this file.

content.user_title: Generate NIH Biosketch Documents for Grant Applications
content.value_statement: NIH grant applications require biosketch documents that follow strict formatting rules defined by the 2022 OMB-approved template. This skill generates compliant DOCX files from structured JSON input and can import publication data from PubMed, saving hours of manual formatting work while ensuring adherence to NIH requirements.
actual_capabilities: Generate NIH-compliant biosketch documents in DOCX format with correct fonts, margins, and section structure
Import publication metadata from PubMed using PMID identifiers via the NCBI E-utilities API
Search PubMed by author name and affiliation to discover relevant publications
Create structured JSON template files with all required NIH biosketch fields for manual data entry
Auto-fill biosketch sections including Personal Statement, Positions and Honors, Contributions to Science, and Research Support
limitations: Requires Python 3.8+ with python-docx and requests packages installed in the environment
PubMed import depends on network access to NCBI servers and may be affected by API rate limits
Limited to the four standard NIH biosketch sections defined in the 2022 OMB-approved format
Cannot independently verify the accuracy or authenticity of user-provided publication and career data

use_cases.title: Grant Application Preparation
use_cases.description: A principal investigator preparing an NIH R01 grant application generates a compliant biosketch from their publication history and career data, ensuring all formatting meets NIH standards before submission.

use_cases.title: Bulk Biosketch Generation for Research Teams
use_cases.description: A research administrator imports publication records from PubMed for multiple team members and generates individual biosketches for a multi-investigator grant proposal.

use_cases.title: First-Time Biosketch Creation
use_cases.description: A graduate student or postdoctoral researcher uses the template generator to create a structured starting point, then fills in their career information to produce their first NIH-compliant biosketch.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Biosketch from Prepared Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an NIH biosketch document from my input data file at data.json. Output the result as my_biosketch.docx. Validate that all required sections are present and the formatting follows the 2022 OMB-approved NIH guidelines.
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic biosketch generation from a pre-filled JSON data file

prompt_templates.title: Create a Template and Fill with Example Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a template JSON file for an NIH biosketch at template.json. Then populate it with realistic example data for a senior researcher in molecular biology, including 3 contributions to science and 2 active research support grants.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting from scratch with template creation and example population

prompt_templates.title: Import Publications by PMID
prompt_templates.prompt: Import publication data from PubMed for PMIDs 12345678, 23456789, and 34567890. Save the results to publications.json, display the titles and authors, and then incorporate these publications into my biosketch under the relevant contributions to science.
prompt_templates.scenario: Auto-importing known publications by their PubMed identifiers

prompt_templates.title: Search PubMed and Curate Results for Biosketch
prompt_templates.prompt: Search PubMed for publications by author 'Smith, John' with affiliation 'Stanford University'. Import the top 50 results, show me the titles and years so I can select the most relevant ones, then generate a biosketch that includes the selected publications under my contributions to science.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow combining author search, manual curation, and biosketch generation

output_examples.input: Load data from researcher.json with personal info, 3 contributions, and 5 publications
output_examples.output: Generated biosketch.docx with NIH-compliant formatting: Arial 11pt font, 0.5 inch margins on all sides, centered header with researcher name and contact information, followed by the four standard NIH sections (A. Personal Statement, B. Positions and Honors, C. Contribution to Science, D. Research Support). Publication references properly formatted under each contribution.

output: Successfully retrieved 2 publications from PubMed API.
Publication 1: 'Mechanisms of Gene Regulation in Eukaryotic Cells' (2024, Journal of Molecular Biology) - 5 authors including the queried researcher
Publication 2: 'Advances in CRISPR-Cas9 Applications for Disease Modeling' (2023, Nature Methods) - 8 authors
Data saved to publications.json with full author lists, journal information, publication years, and DOIs.

output_examples.input: Generate a blank NIH biosketch template file
output: Created template.json with all NIH biosketch sections pre-structured.

Includes: personal_info object (name, position, department, organization, email, eRA Commons ID), positions_and_honors array template, contributions array with description and publication slots, research_support array with grant fields, and publications array with full citation structure.
File is ready for manual data entry or programmatic population.

best_practices: Prepare your input data in JSON format with all required fields completed before running the generation script to minimize back-and-forth iterations
Verify publication details after PubMed import since API-returned metadata may contain formatting inconsistencies or incomplete author lists
Review the generated DOCX file against the latest NIH biosketch format instructions at grants.nih.gov before submitting your grant application
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill to generate biosketches with fabricated, incomplete, or unverified publication data for actual NIH submissions
Do not skip manual review of the generated document: automatic formatting may require adjustments for specific NIH institute requirements
Do not include more than 4 contributions or excessive description text that would cause the document to exceed the NIH 5-page limit

faq.question: What NIH biosketch format does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill supports the 2022 OMB-approved NIH biosketch format with sections A through D (Personal Statement, Positions and Honors, Contributions to Science, and Research Support). It uses Arial 11pt font and 0.5 inch margins as specified by NIH guidelines.

faq.question: Can I import publications from sources other than PubMed?
faq.answer: The skill currently supports PubMed import by PMID and author search. For publications from other databases, you can manually enter the publication data in the JSON input file under the publications or contributions sections.

faq.question: What Python packages are required to run this skill?
faq.answer: You need Python 3.8 or later with two packages: python-docx for generating DOCX files and requests for PubMed API integration. Install both with: pip install python-docx requests.

faq.question: Does the skill validate the accuracy of imported publication data?
faq.answer: The skill retrieves publication metadata directly from the NIH PubMed API but does not independently verify accuracy. Always review imported author lists, titles, and journal information before using them in a grant application.

faq.question: Can I customize the output formatting beyond NIH standards?
faq.answer: The generated document follows NIH standard formatting rules. After generation, you can open and edit the DOCX file in Microsoft Word, LibreOffice, or Google Docs to make additional adjustments.

faq.question: How many contributions to science can I include in one biosketch?
faq.answer: NIH allows up to 4 contributions to science per biosketch. The skill enforces this limit and automatically includes only the first 4 entries from your input data to stay within NIH guidelines.
